era_context: The early-to-mid 13th century is dominated by the Mongol expansion under Genghis Khan's successors, the fragmentation of the Islamic world, the persistence of Crusader states in the Levant, and the consolidation of European feudal kingdoms. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty faces increasing pressure from Mongol forces, and the Song Dynasty controls the south. This era is marked by unprecedented military mobility, technological exchange, and the beginning of long-distance trade networks. The year 1236 falls in the period immediately preceding the major Mongol invasions of Russia and the final conquest of the Jin Dynasty.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia (limited European sources; local chronicles underrepresented)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in European tradition)
Central Asia (limited documentation for this specific year)

events.title: Mongol kurultai (assembly) convenes to plan western campaigns
events.description: A major Mongol kurultai (assembly) convenes to plan the western campaigns that will result in the invasion of Russia and the conquest of Eastern Europe. Batu Khan, grandson of Genghis Khan, is appointed to lead the western forces. The kurultai discusses strategy, allocates resources, and coordinates with other Mongol commanders. This assembly marks the formal beginning of the planning phase for the Russian invasion, which will commence in 1237.
key_figures: Batu Khan (appointed commander of western forces)
events.certainty_note: The kurultai is attested in Persian sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Historiographic consensus places it in 1235–1236, with 1236 being a conventional assignment.

events.title: Mongol forces consolidate control of the Caucasus and southern steppes
events.description: Mongol forces under Batu Khan consolidate control of the Caucasus region and the southern steppes, establishing supply lines and bases for the planned invasion of Russia. The Mongols subjugate or destroy local kingdoms and principalities, including the Circassian and Alanian peoples. This consolidation is essential to the success of the Russian invasion, as it secures the Mongols' southern flank and establishes the logistical infrastructure necessary for the conquest of Russia.
events.certainty_note: Persian sources attest to Mongol consolidation in the Caucasus region in the mid-1230s, though the precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain.

events.title: Jin Dynasty faces intensified Mongol pressure in northern China
events.description: In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty continues to face intensified pressure from Mongol forces under Möngke Khan. The Jin Dynasty, already weakened by decades of Mongol pressure and internal strife, loses further territory. The Jin capital at Kaifeng faces increasing military threats. This campaign is part of the broader Mongol conquest of China that will result in the complete subjugation of the Jin Dynasty by 1234.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Shiji and later Chinese historiographic compilations

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Mongol conquest in northern China is well-established, though precise dating of individual campaigns in 1236 is less certain.

events.title: Ayyubid Sultanate faces internal political instability
events.description: The Ayyubid Sultanate continues to face internal political instability as various branches of the Ayyubid family compete for power and territory. The sultanate is fragmented among multiple rulers, each controlling different regions. This fragmentation weakens the Islamic world's ability to resist Crusader incursions and creates opportunities for the rise of the Mamluk military elite. The political instability in 1236 sets the stage for the eventual Mamluk takeover in 1250.
events.certainty_note: Islamic sources document the Ayyubid fragmentation, though the precise political dynamics in 1236 are less clearly delineated than in later years.

disconfirming_evidence: The kurultai of 1236 is not definitively dated in primary sources; some sources place it in 1235 or 1237. The significance of 1236 as a discrete historical turning point is limited; it marks the planning phase for the Russian invasion rather than a major military or political event. The Jin Dynasty's decline in 1236 is part of a gradual process spanning decades, not a dramatic collapse in a single year. The Ayyubid political instability is a chronic condition throughout the 13th century, not a specific crisis in 1236.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1236 is sparse compared to 1237–1238. Persian sources provide information on Mongol planning and campaigns, but with less precision for this specific year. Russian chronicles do not document events in 1236, as the Mongol invasion had not yet begun. Islamic sources provide limited information on the Ayyubid political situation in 1236. Chinese sources document the Jin Dynasty's decline, but with less detail for individual years. The historical record for 1236 is heavily dependent on later compilations and historiographic consensus rather than contemporary sources. The perspective is that of literate elites in the Islamic world and East Asia; European documentation is minimal for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Mongol kurultai in 1236 led directly to the invasion of Russia in 1237
The consolidation of the Caucasus in 1236 enabled the invasion of Russia in 1237
Mongol pressure on the Jin Dynasty in 1236 contributes to the final collapse in 1234
The Ayyubid instability in 1236 sets the stage for the Mamluk takeover in 1250
